stem: A 5-week-old first-born male is brought to the ED with a 10-day history of vomiting after every feed, now forceful and arching over the shoulder. The vomit is milky, never green or blood-stained. He is hungry immediately after vomiting and feeds vigorously. Birth was 39+4 NVD, weight 3.6 kg. Today his weight is 3.5 kg (50th to 3rd centile cross), capillary refill 3 seconds, anterior fontanelle slightly sunken. A 1 cm mobile firm mass is palpable in the right epigastrium after a test feed, with visible peristalsis crossing the upper abdomen left to right. Venous blood gas: pH 7.51, pCO2 50 mmHg, HCO3 38 mmol/L, Na 134, K 3.1, Cl 89, glucose 4.2 mmol/L. The paediatric surgical registrar wants to know the next step.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 44, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 98%, weight 3.5 kg.
data_table.Examination: Sunken fontanelle, CRT 3 s, mucous membranes dry. Soft abdomen, palpable olive RUQ, visible peristalsis.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.51, pCO2 50, HCO3 38, Na 134, K 3.1, Cl 89, glucose 4.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Transfer to theatre for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after a single saline bolus, with electrolyte correction deferred to the postoperative period
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is never a surgical emergency; operating on an alkalotic, hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic infant risks post-anaesthetic apnoea (compensatory CSF alkalosis). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis

options.text: Resuscitate with 0.9% saline, then maintenance and replacement with 0.45% saline plus 5% dextrose plus potassium until Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30, K over 3.5 mmol/L; confirmatory ultrasound; then pyloromyotomy
options.rationale: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before pyloromyotomy. AU-recommended targets are Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30, K over 3.5 mmol/L. Ultrasound (muscle thickness over 3 mm, channel length over 14 mm) confirms diagnosis, per RCH CPG and Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis
Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

options.text: Commence omeprazole orally and review in clinic in one week as outpatient reflux management
options.rationale: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with palpable olive and hypochloraemic alkalosis is pyloric stenosis, not GORD. Would be correct in mild reflux without electrolyte disturbance (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis

options.text: Request urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation before any fluid resuscitation or surgical referral
options.rationale: Vomiting is non-bilious; upper GI contrast is for suspected malrotation with bilious vomiting. Would be correct with bilious vomiting (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis

options.text: Trial extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 weeks as cow's milk protein allergy is the most likely diagnosis
options.rationale: CMPA does not produce hypochloraemic alkalosis or palpable olive. Would be correct with blood-streaked stools and dermatitis (Source: ASCIA).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis

explanation.summary: Pyloric stenosis (3-6 weeks, male predominant 4:1, first-born) produces hypertrophy of the pyloric muscle, gastric outlet obstruction, and loss of HCl in vomit. The resulting hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before pyloromyotomy.
key_points: Classic age 3-6 weeks, M:F 4:1, first-born predominance
Non-bilious projectile vomiting, hungry vomiter, weight loss
VBG: low Cl, low K, high HCO3, alkalotic pH
USS criteria: muscle thickness greater than 3 mm AND channel length greater than 14 mm
Correct fluids and electrolytes BEFORE theatre to avoid post-op apnoea
explanation.pearls: If the infant is alkalotic, do not let them go to theatre. The anaesthetist will hand them back.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Pyloric Stenosis
Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

subtopic_detail: Infant with non-bilious projectile vomiting and electrolyte disturbance

stem: A 14-month-old previously well boy presents with an 8-hour history of episodic screaming in 15-20 minute cycles, drawing his knees up to his chest, with pallor and limpness between episodes. His mother describes one stool 90 minutes ago that was dark red and mucousy, like jam. There has been one episode of non-bilious vomiting. He had a viral URTI 5 days ago. There has been no foreign travel and no unwell contacts. On examination he is now quiet and pale, HR 152, CRT 3 seconds, T 37.1 degC. The abdomen is soft with a vague sausage-shaped mass palpable in the right upper quadrant and an empty right iliac fossa. There is no peritonism, no guarding. Bedside ultrasound performed by the paediatric registrar shows a target sign in the right upper quadrant with concentric rings of bowel wall. A surgical registrar and an interventional radiologist are both immediately available.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 152 bpm, RR 32, BP 92/58, T 37.1 degC, SpO2 99%, CRT 3 s.
data_table.Examination: Pale, lethargic between episodes. Sausage mass RUQ, empty RIF (Dance sign). No peritonism, no guarding.
data_table.Investigations: POCUS: target sign RUQ, concentric rings of bowel-within-bowel.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient paediatric review the following day given the absence of frank peritonism on examination
options.rationale: Confirmed intussusception with red-currant stool requires immediate reduction; delay risks ischaemia and perforation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception

options.text: Resuscitate with IV fluid and antibiotics, NG decompression, air-contrast enema with surgery on standby
options.rationale: Pneumatic (air) enema is the first-line treatment in haemodynamically stable children without peritonism, with success rates of 75-90% in Australian centres. Resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, NG decompression and surgical standby for the 1% risk of perforation during reduction are mandatory (RCH CPG; RANZCR Paediatric Imaging).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception
RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy for open manual reduction without attempting radiological reduction first
options.rationale: Surgery is for failed enema, peritonism, perforation, or pathological lead point; no peritonism here and air enema has higher success/lower morbidity. Would be correct with peritonism or failed enema (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception

options.text: Perform contrast CT abdomen to confirm the diagnosis before any therapeutic intervention is undertaken
options.rationale: US is gold-standard; CT adds radiation and delay without changing management. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty on US (Source: RANZCR; RCH CPG).
source_refs: RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines
RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception

options.text: Admit for observation overnight on clear fluids and re-image with ultrasound the following morning
options.rationale: Conservative observation of confirmed ileocolic intussusception causes progressive ischaemia; reduction within 24 h has best success. Would never be correct in confirmed cases (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception (6 months to 2 years, peak 5-9 months) typically follows a viral illness with Peyer patch hypertrophy as the lead point. Triad of colicky pain, vomiting and red-currant stool is the late presentation. Diagnosis is by USS (target/donut sign on cross-section, pseudokidney sign longitudinally) and reduction is by pneumatic (air) enema.
key_points: Peak age 5-9 months; consider pathological lead point if outside 3 months to 5 years (e.g. Meckel diverticulum, lymphoma, HSP)
Triad (pain, vomiting, blood per rectum) present in less than 50%
USS first-line; air enema first-line treatment
Air enema success 75-90% in Australia; perforation rate less than 1%
Surgery if peritonism, perforation, failed enema or pathological lead point
explanation.pearls: Recurrence after successful enema reduction occurs in approximately 10% and is usually within 24-48 hours. Discharge planning includes return-precaution counselling.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception
RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Toddler with intermittent colicky pain and bloody stool

stem: A 38-hour-old term male born at 39+5 by NVD to a G2P1 mother with an uneventful pregnancy is brought to the special care nursery by the midwife. He has had three episodes of vomiting in the last 90 minutes, the most recent two clearly bilious (bright green) and photographed on the midwife's phone for the registrar to review. He passed meconium at 18 hours of age and has been breastfeeding poorly since the second feed, latching but suckling weakly and falling asleep. Birth weight 3.4 kg, current weight 3.25 kg, representing a 4% drop. On examination he is irritable with HR 178, RR 62, CRT 3 seconds, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% in room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen without obvious mass, soft, with sluggish bowel sounds. There is no inguinal hernia, no anal stenosis, and no perianal abnormality. Antenatal scans had not commented on intra-abdominal anatomy beyond reporting a normal anatomy scan at 20 weeks, with no polyhydramnios noted. An NG tube is placed and immediately drains 25 mL of bilious fluid with relief of distension. The neonatal registrar has IV access through a peripheral cannula and is preparing maintenance fluids and a 10 mL/kg saline bolus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 62, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% RA, CRT 3 s, weight 3.25 kg.
data_table.Examination: Irritable, mildly distended abdomen, soft, no mass, sluggish bowel sounds. No hernia, normal anus.
data_table.Investigations: NG aspirate 25 mL bilious. IV access x 1, fluids being prepared.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Upper GI contrast study within the next hour, paediatric surgical team aware and theatre standby
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation, showing an abnormal duodeno-jejunal junction position and a corkscrew or beak appearance if volvulus is present. This must occur with surgical standby because volvulus requires laparotomy within minutes to hours to prevent loss of the midgut (RCH CPG; ANZNN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Conservative management with NG decompression and IV fluids overnight, with reassessment in the morning
options.rationale: Watch-and-wait for bilious neonatal vomiting can result in complete midgut loss within 4-6 h of established volvulus. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Malrotation).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound looking for the whirlpool sign before considering further imaging today
options.rationale: USS whirlpool sign is supportive but absence does not exclude malrotation; upper GI contrast is definitive. Would be correct only as adjunct, not gold-standard (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Plain supine abdominal radiograph alone to exclude duodenal atresia as the diagnosis
options.rationale: Plain AXR may show double-bubble or paucity of distal gas but is insufficient to confirm/exclude malrotation. Would be correct only as initial screen with upper GI to follow (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Contrast enema looking for microcolon to exclude meconium ileus as diagnosis
options.rationale: Meconium ileus is a differential but in a term infant with meconium passed at 18 h, malrotation is more likely. Would be correct only with strong meconium ileus features (CF family history, microcolon suspicion) (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. The midgut twists around a narrow mesenteric pedicle and can necrose within hours. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation. The corrective operation is a Ladd procedure: counter-clockwise detorsion, division of Ladd bands, broadening of the mesentery, appendicectomy, and placement of small bowel on the right.
key_points: Any green vomit in the first month is malrotation/volvulus until excluded
Upper GI contrast = gold standard (DJ flexure should be left of midline at the level of pylorus)
USS whirlpool sign is supportive but not definitive
Operation = Ladd procedure
Time from established volvulus to midgut loss may be as short as 4-6 hours
explanation.pearls: If you are ever the most senior person at the bedside of a green-vomiting neonate, your job is to phone the paediatric surgical centre before you have all the answers.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Bilious vomiting in the first 48 hours of life

stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with a 22-hour history of central abdominal pain that migrated to the right iliac fossa 8 hours ago. He has vomited twice, has not eaten since lunch yesterday, and has a temperature of 38.1 deg C. He is reluctant to walk and held his right hip flexed on arrival. There is no diarrhoea, no urinary symptoms, no foreign travel and no sick contacts. He is not on medication and is fully immunised. On examination HR 112, RR 22, BP 108/68, abdomen with localised tenderness at McBurney point, voluntary guarding without rebound, positive cough sign. There is no generalised peritonism. Bloods: WCC 14.8 x10^9/L (neutrophils 11.2), CRP 38 mg/L. Urinalysis is negative. Paediatric Appendicitis Score is 7/10. Radiology offers an ultrasound within the hour.

data_table.Examination: Reluctant to walk, McBurney tenderness, voluntary guarding, positive cough sign, no rebound.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step, and what is the appropriate plan if ultrasound is non-visualising?

options.text: Discharge home with simple analgesia and safety-net advice given the absence of rebound peritonism
options.rationale: PAS 7, WCC 14.8, CRP 38 and migratory pain is high-probability appendicitis; discharge is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Appendicitis).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Acute Appendicitis

options.text: Order contrast CT abdomen and pelvis as the first-line imaging investigation
options.rationale: CT is for diagnostic uncertainty after US or when US is non-visualising in larger children; US is first-line to minimise paediatric radiation. Would be correct only after non-visualising US (Source: RANZCR).
source_refs: RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines
RCH CPG: Acute Appendicitis

options.text: Ultrasound RIF first; if positive (non-compressible blind-ending tubular structure over 6 mm with hyperaemia) proceed to laparoscopic appendicectomy with IV pre-operative antibiotics; if non-visualising, admit for serial clinical reassessment with repeat ultrasound or CT escalation as needed
options.rationale: Ultrasound is first-line paediatric imaging in Australia. A visualised appendix over 6 mm non-compressible with hyperaemia triggers laparoscopic appendicectomy with preoperative ceftriaxone plus metronidazole. Non-visualisation occurs in 20-40 percent and does not exclude appendicitis; serial examination (with or without CT escalation) is the correct next step rather than discharge, per RCH CPG and RANZCR.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Acute Appendicitis
RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

options.text: Treat with IV cefotaxime and metronidazole as antibiotic-only therapy without surgical intervention
options.rationale: Antibiotic-only management is a research approach in selected uncomplicated cases, not standard AU practice; confirmation must precede non-operative trial. Would be correct only in research protocols (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Acute Appendicitis

options.text: Order MRI abdomen and pelvis as the first-line investigation to avoid ionising radiation
options.rationale: MRI is not routinely available out-of-hours and is not first-line; US gives best speed-to-diagnosis. Would be correct only with CT contraindication and US non-visualising (Source: RANZCR).
source_refs: RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

explanation.summary: Paediatric appendicitis diagnosis is clinical plus imaging. The Paediatric Appendicitis Score (PAS) stratifies: 1-3 low (discharge with safety net), 4-6 intermediate (USS, observe), 7-10 high (surgical review, USS if needed, appendicectomy). USS is first-line imaging; CT is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after non-diagnostic USS.
key_points: PAS components: anorexia, nausea/vomiting, migration, RIF tenderness, cough/percussion/heel-tap tenderness, fever greater than or equal to 38, WCC greater than 10, neutrophils greater than 7.5
USS first-line: appendix diameter greater than 6 mm, non-compressible, hyperaemic, periappendiceal fluid
Laparoscopic appendicectomy with preoperative IV antibiotics (e.g. cefazolin + metronidazole per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
Non-visualising USS does not exclude appendicitis; serial exam by paediatric surgical team
CT reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after USS
explanation.pearls: A child who refuses to climb onto the bed or who walks bent over is telling you the diagnosis.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Appendicitis
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Surgical Prophylaxis
RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

subtopic_detail: School-age child with right iliac fossa pain

stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to a regional emergency department at 03:00 with sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 4 hours ago, associated with one episode of vomiting. There is no dysuria, no urethral discharge. He is not sexually active. On examination he is in significant pain, the left hemiscrotum is swollen, erythematous and tender, the left testis is high-riding with a transverse lie, and the cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. Prehn sign is negative (lifting the testis does not relieve pain). The right testis is normal. Urinalysis is negative for leucocytes and nitrites. The nearest paediatric surgical service is 90 minutes away by road. The local on-call general surgeon is in-house. Bedside Doppler ultrasound is not immediately available; the sonographer is on call from home and estimates a 45-minute arrival time.

data_table.Examination: Left hemiscrotum swollen, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn sign.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative. Doppler USS not available for 45 minutes.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Wait for the on-call sonographer to arrive and perform Doppler ultrasound before any surgical decision
options.rationale: Torsion is clinical; US must not delay exploration. Salvage falls from over 90 percent within 6 h to under 10 percent beyond 24 h. Would never be correct in classic torsion presentation (Source: RCH CPG; USANZ).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Scrotum
Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ) Position Statement on Testicular Torsion

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone and doxycycline for presumed bacterial epididymo-orchitis without surgical exploration
options.rationale: Picture (high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn, sudden onset, no urinary symptoms) is torsion not epididymo-orchitis. Antibiotics waste ischaemic time. Would be correct only with epididymo-orchitis features (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Scrotum

options.text: Proceed immediately to scrotal exploration with consent for bilateral orchidopexy or orchidectomy if non-viable
options.rationale: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis warranting immediate surgical exploration. The salvage window is 4-6 hours from onset of pain (currently 4 hours in this scenario). In regional Australia the on-call general surgeon performs exploration rather than transferring; bilateral orchidopexy is standard because the bell-clapper anatomy is bilateral in approximately 80% (RCH CPG; USANZ Position Statement).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Scrotum
Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ) Position Statement on Testicular Torsion

options.text: Attempt manual detorsion in the open-book direction (lateral rotation) then discharge home with urology outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: Manual detorsion may be attempted while preparing theatre but does not replace surgical exploration and fixation. Discharge without fixation risks re-torsion. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: USANZ).
source_refs: Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ) Position Statement on Testicular Torsion

options.text: Transfer the patient by road to the paediatric surgical service 90 minutes away for definitive management
options.rationale: Adding 90 min of transfer when local capability exists pushes total ischaemic time over 6 h. Would be correct only when local paediatric surgical capability is unavailable (Source: USANZ).
source_refs: Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ) Position Statement on Testicular Torsion

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis treated by immediate scrotal exploration. Salvage rates depend on duration of ischaemia: greater than 90% within 6 hours, approximately 50% at 12 hours, less than 10% beyond 24 hours. Bilateral orchidopexy is performed because the bell-clapper deformity is bilateral in approximately 80%.
key_points: Hallmarks: sudden onset pain (often wakes from sleep), high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn
Differentials: epididymo-orchitis (usually febrile, dysuria, gradual onset), torsion of appendix testis (blue dot sign, less severe), incarcerated hernia
Imaging should not delay exploration when the clinical picture is clear
Bilateral orchidopexy at exploration
Salvage rate greater than 90% within 6 hours
explanation.pearls: When the question gives you a 6-hour window and an obvious clinical diagnosis, the wrong answers are usually 'order an imaging test' or 'transfer'.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Scrotum
Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ) Position Statement on Testicular Torsion

subtopic_detail: Adolescent with acute unilateral scrotal pain

stem: A 12-hour-old term female born by elective lower-segment caesarean for breech presentation at 39+2 weeks has had two episodes of bright-green vomiting after attempted breastfeeding. Antenatal scans at 12 and 20 weeks were reported as normal; nuchal translucency was low risk. Birth weight 3.1 kg, Apgars 9 and 9. She has not yet passed meconium. On examination HR 162, RR 56, T 36.8 deg C, SpO2 97% on room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen with a flat lower abdomen and visible epigastric fullness. There is no obvious mass, no inguinal hernia, and the anus is patent on inspection. A nasogastric tube is placed and aspirates 30 mL of bilious fluid. The on-call paediatric registrar is reviewing the supine abdominal radiograph and considering whether the appearance is consistent with duodenal atresia rather than malrotation with midgut volvulus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162, RR 56, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: Upper abdominal distension, flat lower abdomen, patent anus, no hernia. NG bilious aspirate 30 mL.

lead_in: Which radiographic finding most reliably differentiates duodenal atresia from malrotation with midgut volvulus, and what is the implication for next investigation?

options.text: Multiple dilated small bowel loops with air-fluid levels and soap-bubble appearance, suggesting meconium ileus and prompting contrast enema
options.rationale: Soap-bubble (Neuhauser) is meconium ileus, not duodenal atresia or volvulus. Would be correct in meconium ileus (Source: RANZCR).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas, suggesting necrotising enterocolitis
options.rationale: Pneumatosis and portal venous gas are NEC findings, clinically and radiographically distinct. Would be correct in NEC (Source: RCH CPG: NEC).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Double bubble sign with absent distal bowel gas, supporting duodenal atresia; proceed to fluid resuscitation and surgical referral without urgent upper GI contrast
options.rationale: Duodenal atresia produces a classic double bubble (dilated stomach and proximal duodenum) with NO distal bowel gas. In contrast, malrotation with volvulus typically shows some distal gas with a paucity, and the diagnostic test is an URGENT upper GI contrast study showing corkscrew or duodenojejunal junction to the right of midline. Recognising the no-distal-gas double bubble as duodenal atresia therefore obviates upper GI contrast and allows direct fluid resuscitation and surgical referral, whereas any uncertainty mandates urgent contrast study, per RCH CPG.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Free intraperitoneal gas under the diaphragm with a football sign, suggesting perforation
options.rationale: Free gas indicates perforation, not uncomplicated atresia or volvulus. Would be correct in perforation (Source: RANZCR).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Corkscrew appearance of the duodenum visible on the supine plain film
options.rationale: Corkscrew is a fluoroscopic finding on upper GI contrast in malrotation with volvulus, not a plain-film sign. Would be correct on upper GI contrast, not on plain film (Source: RANZCR).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in the neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. The framework of differentials includes malrotation/volvulus, duodenal atresia, jejunal/ileal atresia, meconium ileus (CF), Hirschsprung disease, NEC, sepsis-related ileus, and incarcerated inguinal hernia. Plain radiograph plus upper GI contrast distinguish these.
key_points: Double bubble + no distal gas = duodenal atresia (30% trisomy 21)
Double bubble + distal gas = malrotation with partial obstruction
Multiple dilated loops + soap-bubble = meconium ileus (suspect CF)
Pneumatosis = NEC
Free gas / football sign = perforation
explanation.pearls: If the lower abdomen is scaphoid in a vomiting neonate, suspect a high obstruction; if it is distended, suspect a low obstruction.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Neonate with green vomiting on day 1 - differential framework

stem: A 16-month-old boy of Anglo-Australian background presents with severe constipation since the second week of life. His mother reports he passed meconium at 52 hours of age, required digital stimulation to pass stool for the first 6 months, and was breastfed exclusively until 6 months when solids were introduced uneventfully. He now stools once every 5-7 days, with explosive decompression when finally passing. His growth has crossed centiles downwards from the 50th at birth to the 9th today. He is now febrile at 38.6 degC, lethargic, with foul-smelling green-tinged diarrhoea overnight, mild abdominal distension, and tachycardia at 148. His abdomen is distended and tympanic, with no obvious mass and no organomegaly. PR examination is performed gently by the paediatric registrar and produces an explosive release of gas and liquid stool with relief of distension. He has had multiple aperient trials including macrogol and lactulose without sustained improvement, and dietary fibre optimisation through the maternal and child health nurse. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis or thyroid disease. Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis was negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148, RR 32, T 38.6 degC, weight on 9th centile (was 50th at birth).
data_table.Examination: Distended tympanic abdomen, explosive PR release. Lethargic.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive investigation?

options.text: Sweat chloride test to exclude cystic fibrosis as the cause of meconium delay
options.rationale: CF can cause meconium ileus but does not explain chronic explosive PR decompression or current enterocolitis. Would be correct as adjunct workup in suspected CF (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease

options.text: Anorectal manometry alone as the single definitive diagnostic test for this
options.rationale: Anorectal manometry shows absent RAIR but is technically difficult in young children and supportive only; biopsy is gold-standard. Would be correct as supportive test in older children (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease

options.text: Contrast enema with assessment for transition zone, followed by trial of laxatives
options.rationale: Contrast enema may show transition zone and reversed recto-sigmoid ratio but is supportive only; biopsy is required. Empirical laxatives miss the disease. Would be correct as initial radiological assessment, not definitive (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease

options.text: Full-thickness rectal suction biopsy with assessment for ganglion cells and acetylcholinesterase staining
options.rationale: Rectal biopsy is the gold-standard diagnostic test for Hirschsprung disease, demonstrating absent ganglion cells in the submucosal (Meissner) and myenteric (Auerbach) plexuses with hypertrophied nerve trunks staining positive for acetylcholinesterase. The current presentation is Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis, which carries up to 30% mortality if untreated (RCH CPG; ANZ Paediatric Surgery).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease
Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Paediatric Constipation

options.text: Faecal calprotectin and stool MCS as the initial diagnostic test
options.rationale: Calprotectin is for suspected IBD, not Hirschsprung; stool MCS may identify enterocolitis pathogens but does not diagnose aganglionosis. Would be correct in suspected IBD (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease

explanation.summary: Hirschsprung disease is congenital absence of ganglion cells in the distal bowel due to failed migration of neural crest cells. Suspect in any infant with delayed meconium beyond 48 hours, chronic constipation from birth, or failure-to-thrive. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis is the life-threatening complication.
key_points: Meconium passed greater than 48 hours after birth in approximately 90%
Rectal biopsy is gold standard: absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerves, positive acetylcholinesterase
Contrast enema: transition zone, reversed recto-sigmoid ratio
Definitive treatment: pull-through procedure (Soave, Swenson, Duhamel)
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis: fever, distension, foul diarrhoea; mortality up to 30% if missed
explanation.pearls: An infant who needs digital stimulation to pass meconium is telling you the diagnosis; do not wait for failure-to-thrive.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease
Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Paediatric Constipation

subtopic_detail: Toddler with delayed meconium and chronic constipation

stem: A 28+5 week preterm female born at birth weight 1.05 kg is now on day 11 of life in the neonatal intensive care unit. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and trophic feeds of expressed breast milk were introduced on day 5 and advanced to 60 mL/kg/day by day 10. Over the last 12 hours she has had two large bilious gastric aspirates, abdominal distension increasing from 25 cm to 28.5 cm girth, and one episode of bright red blood in the stool. She has become apnoeic with three desaturations to less than 80% in the last hour requiring stimulation. HR has risen from 145 to 178. T is 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 mmHg with mean arterial pressure 30. CRT is 4 seconds. The abdominal wall is shiny with bluish discolouration over the right flank. WCC has fallen from 9.2 to 4.1 x10^9/L, platelets from 224 to 86, CRP risen from less than 5 to 92 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L. The supine abdominal radiograph shows multiple dilated loops of bowel with intramural gas (pneumatosis intestinalis) and a curvilinear lucency over the liver consistent with portal venous gas. There is no free intraperitoneal gas on a left lateral decubitus film. The paediatric surgical service has been consulted by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 70, T 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 (MAP 30), SpO2 88% on CPAP, CRT 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Distended shiny abdomen with bluish flank discolouration. PR bright red blood.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.1, platelets 86, CRP 92, lactate 4.8. AXR: pneumatosis + portal venous gas, no free gas.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood culture results are available to avoid promoting antibiotic resistance and unnecessary coverage
options.rationale: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes must commence immediately on NEC suspicion. Waiting for cultures delays critical therapy. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Neonatal Sepsis

options.text: Continue enteral feeds at current volume and add oral probiotics to reduce bacterial gut translocation
options.rationale: Established NEC mandates immediate NBM; continuing feeds worsens ischaemic injury. Probiotics are prophylactic in some units but no role in established NEC. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparotomy regardless of radiographic findings of pneumatosis or perforation
options.rationale: Surgery is for perforation (free gas, fixed loop, deterioration despite medical management). Bell IIB without perforation is medical with surgical standby. Would be correct only with perforation or clinical deterioration (Source: RCH CPG NEC).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis

options.text: Nil by mouth, OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, TPN, serial films, paediatric surgical involvement
options.rationale: This is Bell stage IIB NEC (pneumatosis with portal venous gas, no perforation). Management is medical: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics (penicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole per Therapeutic Guidelines), TPN, serial AXR every 6 hours for 48-72 hours to detect perforation. Surgery is indicated for perforation (Bell IIIB) or clinical deterioration (RCH CPG NEC; ANZNN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Neonatal Sepsis

options.text: Place a peritoneal drain at the bedside as definitive treatment without commencing antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Peritoneal drainage is a salvage technique for unstable ELBW with perforation; not a substitute for antibiotics or definitive laparotomy. Would be correct only as salvage in unstable ELBW (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

explanation.summary: Necrotising enterocolitis is a multifactorial inflammatory/ischaemic injury to immature gut, peaking at 28-32 weeks corrected age in the second to third postnatal week. Bell staging: I suspected (non-specific), II definite (IIA pneumatosis, IIB pneumatosis + portal gas), III advanced (IIIA critically ill no perforation, IIIB perforation).
key_points: Peak: 28-32 weeks corrected, day 7-14 of life
Risk factors: prematurity, formula feeding, rapid feed advance, hypoxia, sepsis
Pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic
Management: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics, TPN, serial AXR
Surgery for perforation, fixed dilated loop, abdominal wall erythema/discolouration, or deterioration
explanation.pearls: A shiny abdomen with bluish discolouration is a late sign of full-thickness bowel wall ischaemia and is a call to the operating room.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Neonatal Sepsis

subtopic_detail: Preterm infant with feed intolerance and abdominal distension

stem: A 22-month-old girl is brought to a rural emergency department 70 minutes after her older brother saw her swallow a small shiny disc that he had taken from a household remote control. He believes she put it directly in her mouth and swallowed it. She is now drooling slightly, refuses to drink, has had one episode of non-bloody vomiting but is not in respiratory distress. There is no stridor, no wheeze, no blood in the saliva. HR 134, RR 28, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99% in room air. Examination of the oropharynx is unremarkable; the abdomen is soft and non-tender. The mother brings the empty battery compartment of the remote and the parents identify the missing battery as a CR2032 lithium coin cell (20 mm diameter). The emergency department has plain radiography but no on-site paediatric endoscopy. The nearest paediatric centre with interventional endoscopy is 2 hours by road or 45 minutes by retrieval helicopter (currently available). A chest and abdominal radiograph shows a round radio-opaque disc with a double-rim (halo) appearance at the level of T2-T3 in the oesophagus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 134, RR 28, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Drooling, refuses fluids, no stridor, no respiratory distress.
data_table.Investigations: CXR/AXR: 20 mm disc with double-rim sign at T2-T3 oesophagus.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Transfer by road over 2 hours; no oral honey as child is under 24 months
options.rationale: Honey contraindication is for under-12 months (botulism risk); this child is 22 months. Helicopter is faster than 2-h road transfer for an oesophageal battery. Would be correct only under 12 months (Source: RCH CPG; NHMRC Infant Feeding).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Button Battery Ingestion

options.text: Discharge home with safety-net advice to return if symptoms worsen, given the child appears clinically stable on arrival
options.rationale: Any oesophageal button battery is a surgical emergency regardless of current symptoms; delay risks perforation, TOF and aorto-oesophageal fistula. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; ACCC).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Button Battery Ingestion

options.text: Induce vomiting with oral ipecac syrup at the bedside now to expel the swallowed battery
options.rationale: Induced emesis is contraindicated (aspiration, further oesophageal injury); ipecac is no longer recommended by PIC for any ingestion. Would never be correct (Source: PIC).
source_refs: Poisons Information Centre Australia (13 11 26)

options.text: Wait 4 hours and repeat radiograph to see whether the battery has spontaneously passed the lower oesophageal sphincter
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is unsafe for oesophageal battery; 4-h delay pushes beyond 2-h necrosis threshold. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Button Battery Ingestion

options.text: Activate retrieval for endoscopic battery removal within 2 hours; oral honey en route if age >12 months and airway intact
options.rationale: Button battery in the oesophagus is a time-critical emergency: tissue injury begins within 15-30 minutes and full-thickness necrosis can occur within 2 hours. The Australian Resuscitation Council and RCH CPG recommend honey (or sucralfate) every 10 minutes in children over 12 months with no airway compromise to coat the battery and reduce alkaline injury, while urgent endoscopic removal is arranged. Helicopter retrieval is faster than road (RCH CPG; Poisons Information Centre).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Button Battery Ingestion
Poisons Information Centre Australia (13 11 26)

explanation.summary: Button battery in the oesophagus causes alkaline electrolysis injury within 15-30 minutes and can perforate within 2 hours. Australian guidelines (RCH, Poisons Information Centre, ACCC) recommend immediate endoscopic removal, honey every 10 minutes in children over 12 months while awaiting endoscopy, and notification of PIC on 13 11 26.
key_points: Double-rim (halo) sign on AP radiograph distinguishes battery from coin
Step-off sign on lateral
Oesophageal lodgement = endoscopic removal within 2 hours
Honey 10 mL every 10 minutes (children over 12 months) reduces injury severity
Avoid in infants under 12 months (botulism); avoid if airway compromised
explanation.pearls: Coins are smooth and round and easy to ignore. The double rim on the AP film and step-off on the lateral are how you do not miss the diagnosis.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Button Battery Ingestion
Poisons Information Centre Australia (13 11 26)
ACCC Product Safety Australia - Button Batteries

subtopic_detail: Toddler with witnessed coin-like object ingestion

stem: A 4-month-old formula-fed infant is brought to the GP with a 6-week history of frequent posseting, irritability and back-arching after feeds, and intermittent streaks of fresh blood and mucus in the stool that the mother has photographed on her phone. He has mild atopic eczema on the cheeks and antecubital fossae. He was born at term, weight at birth 3.5 kg (50th centile). At 6 weeks his weight was on the 50th centile; today at 4 months he is on the 9th centile, having crossed two major centiles downward over 10 weeks. He is exclusively fed standard cow-milk-based formula and has never had any breastmilk feeds since the second week of life. There is a family history of asthma in the mother and peanut allergy in an older sibling. He is otherwise well, with no fever, no dehydration, normal urine output, and a normal abdominal examination with no distension or tenderness. The mother has tried thickened formula, smaller more frequent feeds, and head-up positioning after feeds for 30 minutes, without improvement.

data_table.Examination: Mild facial eczema. Soft non-tender abdomen. Weight crossed from 50th to 9th centile over 10 weeks.
data_table.Investigations: Stool with visible blood and mucus streaks. No fever.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral omeprazole daily for a 4-week trial as the most likely diagnosis is GORD-related vomiting
options.rationale: Blood/mucus in stool, eczema, atopic family history and faltering growth are red flags for CMPA, not GORD. PPI trials should not delay elimination diet. Would be correct in uncomplicated GORD without atopy or blood (Source: ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Cow's Milk (Dairy) Allergy Guidelines

options.text: Switch to extensively hydrolysed formula for 2-4 weeks, monitor symptoms/growth, re-challenge to confirm; allergy referral if severe
options.rationale: Suspected non-IgE-mediated CMPA in a formula-fed infant is managed by a trial of extensively hydrolysed formula (eHF) for 2-4 weeks, with reassessment of symptoms and growth, and a re-challenge to confirm. Amino-acid formula is reserved for severe or eHF-non-responsive cases. Diagnosis is by elimination and re-challenge, not allergy testing (ASCIA; RCH CPG).
source_refs: ASCIA Cow's Milk (Dairy) Allergy Guidelines
RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Cow's Milk Protein Allergy

options.text: Switch directly to soy-based formula as the first-line alternative formula without considering hydrolysate today
options.rationale: Soy is not first-line under 6 months (phytoestrogens) and 10-15 percent CMPA cross-reacts. Extensively hydrolysed formula is first-line. Would be correct in older infants after EHF failure (Source: ASCIA; NHMRC).
source_refs: ASCIA Cow's Milk (Dairy) Allergy Guidelines
NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines

options.text: Order skin-prick testing and specific IgE to cow's milk to confirm the diagnosis before any dietary change
options.rationale: Non-IgE-mediated CMPA (the likely subtype) is not detected by IgE/SPT. Diagnosis is by elimination and re-challenge. Would be correct only in suspected IgE-mediated (immediate reactions) (Source: ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Cow's Milk (Dairy) Allergy Guidelines

options.text: Advise the mother to dilute the formula with extra water and add infant gripe water at each feed
options.rationale: Diluting formula reduces caloric/electrolyte intake and worsens faltering growth; gripe water has no role in CMPA. Would never be correct (Source: NHMRC).
source_refs: NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines

explanation.summary: Distinguishing GORD from CMPA in a vomiting infant requires assessment for red flags: blood/mucus in stool, eczema, atopic family history, perianal excoriation, and weight faltering favour CMPA. Uncomplicated GORD without red flags is managed with feed positioning, thickeners and reassurance.
key_points: Non-IgE CMPA: bloody/mucousy stool, eczema, faltering growth, no anaphylactic features; skin-prick/IgE negative
IgE CMPA: urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis within 2 hours of ingestion; skin-prick/IgE positive
First-line formula: extensively hydrolysed (eHF) for 2-4 weeks
Amino-acid formula for severe disease or eHF non-response
Re-challenge to confirm diagnosis; most resolve by 3-5 years
explanation.pearls: Bloody mucus + eczema + crossed centiles in a vomiting infant is CMPA until proven otherwise. PPIs do not fix that.

sources.label: ASCIA Cow's Milk (Dairy) Allergy Guidelines
RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Cow's Milk Protein Allergy
NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Infant with vomiting, eczema and stool changes

stem: A 10-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of poor feeding for the last 24 hours. He is not yet rolling and is not yet sitting. Examination is unremarkable except for a 2 cm bluish bruise over the left cheek and a 1 cm bruise on the right ear pinna. The mother offers no clear mechanism but mentions that her partner reported the baby may have rolled off the couch, although the baby has never previously rolled. On further examination there is a small frenular tear under the upper lip and the anterior fontanelle is full but not tense. He is afebrile with normal vital signs. His older sibling, aged 3, was treated for a spiral femur fracture last year that was attributed to a fall from a slide. The family is known to community services for housing instability. The mother has a history of intimate-partner violence in a previous relationship. The current partner is not the biological father and is not present. Birth was at term, NVD, with no perinatal concerns, and the baby is up to date with immunisations.

data_table.Examination: 2 cm cheek bruise, 1 cm ear pinna bruise, frenular tear, full fontanelle. Pre-mobile.
data_table.Investigations: Pending. Family known to community services.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Notify the police but not the statutory child protection service, as bruising alone does not meet child protection thresholds
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting requires notification to statutory child protection service (DCJ in NSW, DFFH in VIC, etc); police notification is additional not substitute. Would never be correct (Source: AIFS).
source_refs: Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect
DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide

options.text: Reassure and discharge home with feeding advice, given that the bruises are small and the mother provided a plausible explanation today
options.rationale: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant is a red flag; TEN-4 FACES mandates investigation. Discharge without workup misses ~30 percent of subsequent severe abuse. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; AIFS).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Child Abuse
Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect

options.text: Order coagulation studies in isolation to exclude haemophilia and discharge if all results return normal
options.rationale: Coagulation studies are part of workup but cannot replace skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and notification. Bleeding disorders and NAI are not mutually exclusive. Would never be correct alone (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Child Abuse

options.text: Admit for full child protection workup (skeletal survey, CT, fundoscopy, FBC, coags, LFTs, urine drug screen) and notify statutory service
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury for non-accidental injury (TEN-4 FACES rule: bruising on Torso, Ears, Neck, or any bruising in a child 4 months or younger). Multiple bruises plus a frenular tear plus an unwitnessed mechanism plus a sibling with a suspicious historic fracture meet threshold for mandatory reporting and a full medical workup including skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and coagulation panel (RCH CPG Child Abuse; AIFS; DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Child Abuse
Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect
DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide

options.text: Wait 48 hours to see if the bruising evolves clinically before deciding whether to make any report
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting acts on reasonable belief, not certainty; waiting risks further injury and breaches legal threshold. Would never be correct (Source: AIFS).
source_refs: Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect

explanation.summary: The TEN-4 FACES rule (Pierce 2010, validated 2021) identifies sentinel bruising patterns: bruising on Torso, Ear, or Neck in any child 4 years or younger, OR any bruising in an infant 4 months or younger, OR bruising in the FACES regions (Frenulum, Auricular, Cheek, Eye, Sclera). Workup: skeletal survey, CT head, fundoscopy, FBC, coagulation, LFTs, lipase, urine drug screen, social/safety assessment.
key_points: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant requires NAI workup
TEN-4 FACES: T (torso) E (ear) N (neck) under 4 yr; any bruise under 4 months; FACES regions
Frenular tear in pre-mobile infant is classic NAI sentinel
Mandatory notification to statutory child protection service (DCJ NSW or jurisdictional equivalent)
Mandatory reporters have legal immunity for good-faith reports
explanation.pearls: If a baby cannot roll, they cannot bruise themselves. Document the absence of a developmental match between mechanism and injury.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Child Abuse
Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect
DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide

stem: A 15-year-old girl attends a GP clinic alone requesting the combined oral contraceptive pill. She is sexually active with one partner who is also 15 and is in the same year at school. She has had a normal menarche at 12, regular cycles every 28-30 days, no medical comorbidities, no migraine with aura of VTE, and a normal blood pressure of 110/68. Her BMI is 22. She does not want her parents told because she is worried they will react badly even though she trusts them on most other matters. She lives at home with both parents and an older sister, attends school regularly, is performing well academically, plays competitive netball, and has no signs of coercion, grooming, intimate-partner violence, mental illness, or substance misuse on a HEEADSSS assessment conducted by the GP at this consultation. She asks the GP directly whether the GP must tell her parents, and demonstrates a clear understanding of how the pill works, the failure rate, the risk of VTE, and the importance of barrier protection for STI prevention. Her partner is not present and has not been mentioned in any concerning way.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate response by the GP?

options.text: Defer all decisions until a Medicare-rebated specialist adolescent gynaecology review, which typically takes 3 to 6 months in public clinics
options.rationale: GPs are appropriately trained to prescribe contraception to competent adolescents; delay exposes the patient to unintended pregnancy. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health
Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

options.text: Decline to prescribe and require the patient to return with a parent before any consideration of contraception
options.rationale: Mandating parental involvement breaches the right to confidential health care and drives patients away. Gillick competence allows confidential prescribing. Would be correct only if competence not established (Source: RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health

options.text: Mandatorily notify DCJ because any sexual activity by a 15-year-old is reportable in all Australian jurisdictions
options.rationale: NSW age of consent is 16, but consensual sex between two 15-year-olds of similar maturity without exploitation/coercion does not automatically meet mandatory reporting threshold. Would be correct with significant age gap or exploitation (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
source_refs: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health

options.text: Prescribe the COCP but inform the parents by telephone after the consultation for safety reasons and ongoing supervision
options.rationale: Disclosure to parents without consent breaches confidentiality; reserved for risk-of-significant-harm scenarios the patient cannot/will not address. Would never be correct as routine (Source: RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health
AMA Code of Ethics

options.text: Assess Gillick competence, document understanding, prescribe COCP without parental notification, encourage but do not require disclosure to a trusted adult
options.rationale: Under the High Court of Australia decision in Marion's Case (1992), adapting the UK Gillick principle, a mature minor with sufficient understanding and intelligence to fully comprehend a proposed treatment may consent without parental involvement. Contraception in a 15-year-old in a non-coercive relationship of similar age, with no safety concerns, meets the standard for confidential prescribing (RACGP; AMA Code of Ethics).
source_refs: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health
AMA Code of Ethics

explanation.summary: Gillick competence (or mature minor doctrine, as applied in Australia after Marion's Case) allows a young person to consent to treatment without parental involvement if they have sufficient understanding and intelligence to fully appreciate what is proposed. Confidentiality is the default; it is breached only when risk of significant harm cannot be otherwise managed.
key_points: Mature minor doctrine: capacity-based, not age-based
HEEADSSS screen: Home, Education/Employment, Eating, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/Mood, Safety
Confidentiality is the default; explain limits at the start of every adolescent consultation
Limits: risk of significant harm to self or others, including coercion, grooming, or significant age gap
Document the competence assessment in detail
explanation.pearls: Explain confidentiality and its limits at the start of every adolescent consultation. That sentence does more for engagement than anything else you say.

sources.label: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health
AMA Code of Ethics
Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to a paediatric outpatient clinic with a 4-month history of crampy lower abdominal pain that is worse after meals, non-bloody loose stool 4 to 6 times daily including overnight episodes that wake him from sleep, intermittent low-grade fevers, recurrent painful mouth ulcers on the inner lip, and 5 kg of weight loss. He has fallen from the 75th to the 25th height centile over the last year and pubertal development is at Tanner 2 when his peers are at Tanner 3 to 4. There is a paternal uncle with Crohn disease and a maternal grandmother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination he is pale, BMI 17, with mild RIF tenderness, no palpable mass, a perianal skin tag, and a small fistulous opening at the 7 o'clock position with no active discharge. Bloods show Hb 102 g/L, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 8 microg/L, platelets 482, CRP 48 mg/L, ESR 56 mm/hr, albumin 28 g/L. Faecal calprotectin is 1850 microg/g (reference less than 50). Stool MCS and PCR for bacterial pathogens, C. difficile and parasites are negative. Coeliac serology is negative with normal total IgA. He is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, RR 18, BP 110/70, T 37.4 degC, BMI 17.
data_table.Examination: Pale, RIF tender, perianal skin tag, fistulous opening at 7 o'clock.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102, MCV 76, ferritin 8, platelets 482, CRP 48, albumin 28. Faecal calprotectin 1850 microg/g. Coeliac neg, stool MCS neg.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Trial of empirical 6-week elimination diet before any invasive endoscopic investigation today
options.rationale: Exclusive enteral nutrition is first-line induction for Crohn's, but is started AFTER endoscopic confirmation, not as substitute. Would be correct after diagnosis confirmed (Source: RCH CPG IBD; ESPGHAN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
eviQ Paediatric Inflammatory Bowel Disease Protocols

options.text: Empirical 4-week course of oral metronidazole then reassessment of faecal calprotectin levels
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics without endoscopy delay diagnosis and risk masking activity; stool studies are already negative. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG IBD).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Inflammatory Bowel Disease

options.text: Capsule endoscopy as the first-line investigation to assess the small bowel mucosa
options.rationale: Capsule endoscopy is for suspected isolated small bowel disease after negative ileocolonoscopy plus small bowel imaging; contraindicated where stricture risk exists. Would be correct only after negative initial endoscopy and imaging (Source: ESPGHAN).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
ESPGHAN Revised Porto Criteria for Paediatric IBD

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as the imaging modality of choice for diagnosis
options.rationale: MR enterography is preferred over CT in paediatric IBD to minimise lifetime radiation. Would be correct only if MR unavailable and urgent imaging needed (Source: RANZCR; eviQ).
source_refs: eviQ Paediatric Inflammatory Bowel Disease Protocols

options.text: Ileocolonoscopy with terminal ileal and colonic segmental biopsies plus upper GI endoscopy with MR enterography
options.rationale: The clinical picture (perianal fistula, mouth ulcers, growth failure, raised inflammatory markers, faecal calprotectin greater than 250 microg/g, family history) is highly suggestive of paediatric Crohn disease. The Porto criteria recommend ileocolonoscopy plus upper GI endoscopy with biopsies plus small-bowel imaging (MR enterography preferred over CT for radiation reduction) for diagnosis and disease mapping (RCH CPG IBD; eviQ Paediatric IBD; ESPGHAN Porto criteria).
source_refs: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
eviQ Paediatric Inflammatory Bowel Disease Protocols
ESPGHAN Revised Porto Criteria for Paediatric IBD

explanation.summary: Paediatric IBD diagnosis follows the ESPGHAN revised Porto criteria: ileocolonoscopy + upper GI endoscopy + small bowel imaging (MR enterography preferred). Faecal calprotectin greater than 250 microg/g has high sensitivity for mucosal inflammation and is used to triage who needs endoscopy. Induction in Crohn is exclusive enteral nutrition or corticosteroids; biologics (infliximab/adalimumab) for severe or fistulising disease per eviQ.
key_points: Faecal calprotectin greater than 250 microg/g = likely organic inflammation
Porto criteria: ileocolonoscopy + OGD + small bowel imaging
MR enterography preferred over CT in children
Crohn induction: exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) 6-8 weeks, mucosal healing in approx 80%
Biologics per eviQ for moderate-severe Crohn, fistulising disease, or steroid-dependent disease
explanation.pearls: Perianal disease in an adolescent + mouth ulcers + growth faltering is Crohn until proven otherwise. Look in the mouth and look at the perianal skin.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
eviQ Paediatric Inflammatory Bowel Disease Protocols
ESPGHAN Revised Porto Criteria for Paediatric IBD

subtopic_detail: Adolescent with chronic diarrhoea and weight loss

stem: A 22-month-old girl is referred by her maternal and child health nurse for faltering growth. Her birth weight was on the 75th centile after an uncomplicated term pregnancy and NVD. She was exclusively breastfed until 6 months when solids were introduced including wheat-based cereals, pasta and bread, with normal progression of feeding milestones over the next several months. She is now on the 9th centile for weight and 25th for height, having crossed two major weight centiles downward over the last 8 months. Her mother reports chronic loose, bulky, pale, foul-smelling stools 3 to 5 times daily that float in the toilet, marked abdominal distension that develops over the course of the day, irritability particularly in the late afternoon, and a recent reduction in appetite. The paternal grandmother has type 1 diabetes mellitus and an older sibling aged 7 has confirmed coeliac disease diagnosed two years ago on duodenal biopsy. On examination today she is pale, thin, with mild abdominal distension, no organomegaly, no clubbing, and reduced subcutaneous fat over the buttocks and proximal thighs. The GP orders anti-tTG IgA and total IgA as the initial screen. Anti-tTG IgA returns at 230 U/mL (reference less than 20, more than 10 times upper limit), total IgA is 1.6 g/L (normal for age, excluding IgA deficiency), endomysial antibody is positive, and HLA-DQ2 is positive. The mother attends to discuss the results and asks the GP what should happen next. She is keen to start a strict gluten-free diet immediately because she has already done so for the older sibling and knows the supermarket products.

data_table.Examination: Pale, thin, distended abdomen, reduced buttock fat pads, no organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: anti-tTG IgA 230 U/mL (less than 20), total IgA 1.6 g/L (normal), EMA positive, HLA-DQ2 positive.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue gluten, refer paediatric gastroenterology for duodenal biopsy, only start gluten-free after histology; baseline nutritional studies; screen relatives
options.rationale: Australian and ESPGHAN guidelines require duodenal biopsy in symptomatic children with positive serology before commencing a gluten-free diet, unless the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway is fully met (anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit of normal AND positive EMA on a separate sample AND HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive) and this is delivered in a specialist paediatric gastroenterology setting with full counselling. Starting a gluten-free diet before biopsy can invalidate the histology if the family later wants definitive diagnosis. First-degree relative screening is recommended (RACGP; Coeliac Australia; ESPGHAN).
source_refs: RACGP Coeliac Disease Position Statement
Coeliac Australia Diagnosis Guidelines
ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

options.text: Commence a strict gluten-free diet immediately given the strongly positive serology and family history, with no further investigation needed
options.rationale: Initiating GFD before biopsy in Australia is not recommended outside the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway with full criteria met. Would be correct only under specialist no-biopsy pathway with two-sample criteria (Source: ESPGHAN; RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP Coeliac Disease Position Statement
Coeliac Australia Diagnosis Guidelines

options.text: Discontinue gluten for 4 weeks then perform a gluten challenge with repeat serology and biopsy
options.rationale: Withdrawing gluten before biopsy makes diagnostic biopsy unreliable; gluten challenge is for prior inadequate evidence or diagnostic uncertainty. Would be correct only after prior GFD started without diagnosis (Source: ESPGHAN).
source_refs: ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

options.text: Repeat anti-tTG IgA in 6 months and only proceed to further investigation if titres remain elevated
options.rationale: Symptomatic child with strongly positive serology, positive EMA and faltering growth must not have diagnosis delayed; treatment drives growth recovery. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP Coeliac Disease Position Statement
Coeliac Australia Diagnosis Guidelines

options.text: Order an IgG-based gliadin antibody panel and faecal fat studies as the next investigations
options.rationale: IgG anti-gliadin has been superseded by anti-tTG/EMA; faecal fat is rarely used in modern coeliac diagnosis. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP; ESPGHAN).
source_refs: RACGP Coeliac Disease Position Statement
ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

explanation.summary: Coeliac disease in children: screen with anti-tTG IgA plus total IgA (false negative if IgA deficient). Positive serology in a symptomatic child warrants duodenal biopsy via paediatric gastroenterology to confirm Marsh-Oberhuber 2 or higher histology. The ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway requires anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit on one sample, positive EMA on a separate sample, HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive, and a specialist setting. Do not start a gluten-free diet before diagnosis is secured.
key_points: Screen: anti-tTG IgA + total IgA; if IgA deficient use IgG-based serology
Positive serology + symptoms = paediatric gastroenterology referral for biopsy
ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway: anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x ULN + EMA positive on second sample + HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive
Maintain gluten in diet until diagnosis confirmed
Screen first-degree relatives; monitor growth, iron, vitamin D, calcium, thyroid function and bone density
explanation.pearls: The most common reason a coeliac diagnosis becomes uncertain is starting the gluten-free diet too early. Wait for the biopsy or for the full no-biopsy criteria.

sources.label: RACGP Coeliac Disease Position Statement
Coeliac Australia Diagnosis Guidelines
ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020

subtopic_detail: Toddler with faltering growth and loose stools
